coredata.dc:title: Engineering of upconversion carbon dots/metal-organic frameworks “Peeled Pitaya-Like” heterostructure for mitochondria-targeted photodynamic therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Xiang, Qin
Li, Wei
Tan, Yan
Shi, Jianwei
Dong, Mingjie
Cheng, Jiale
Huang, Jingkun
Zhang, Weiyun
Gong, Youcong
Yang, Qiqi
Yang, Lingzhi
Dong, Haifeng
Zhang, Xueji
coredata.dc:description: Developing of near-infrared (NIR) light triggered metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) nano-photosensitizers (nPSs) is highly desirable for photodynamic therapy (PDT) owing to its promising properties. Herein, we designed a mitochondria-targeted, 808 nm near-infrared (NIR) light-triggered upconversion carbon dots (CDs) coordinated porphyrinic MOFs (PCDs) nPSs for enhanced PDT. The system was fabricated through incorporation of sufficient ultrasmall CDs into the nanopores as well as the surface of porphyrinic porous MOFs to construct a peeled pitaya-like heterostructure, which was further functionalized with mitochondria-targeted moieties of triphenylphosphines (TPPs), termed PCDTs, for enhanced PDT. The upconversion CDs act as tremendous “antennas” to efficiently absorb NIR laser, and the tight coupling between the CDs and porphyrin in MOFs, greatly shortened the distance between the two components and improved the efficiency of the energy transfer from the CDs to porphyrinic MOFs, thus yielded a sharply enhanced 1O2 generation capability. The mitochondria-targeted ability further enhanced the ROS oxidative stress, accelerated dysfunction of mitochondria, and induced intrinsic apoptosis. Excellent anticancer ability was demonstrated by the in vitro cancer cells killing and in vivo suppression of tumor growth. This work presents a novel engineered heterostructure to combat the current limitation of MOF in PDT.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vivo imaging and biodistribution analysis
Synthesis and characterization of PCDs and PCDTs
NIR-triggered energy transfer and according mechanism explanation
Estimation of ROS generation, mitochondrial targeting and damage
The biosafety, biodistribution and fluorescence imaging of PCDTs
In vivo tumor therapy studies and histological analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthetic process of PCDs and PCDTs. HRTEM images of (b) PCDs and (d) CDs. (c) Enlargement of the selected area of (b). (e) DLS size distribution and (f) Zeta potentials of as-made CDs, PCN-224, PCDs and PCDTs in aqueous solution (pH = 7.4) (g) FT-IR spectra of CDs, PCN-224, PCDs, PCDTs and TPP. (h) HRTEM image and corresponding area-elemental mapping of PCDs.

(a) UV − vis absorption spectrum of the PCN-224 and UCL spectrum of CDs. The overlap is indicated in blue. (b) UCL decay curves of CDs and PCDs. (c) NIR light-triggered 1O2 generation by CDs, PCN-224. PCDs and PCDTs determined by SOSG assay. (d) ESR spectra of PCDs with or without 808 nm laser irradiation for characterization of 1O2. (e) N2 adsorption isotherms and (f) pore volume of PCN-224 and PCDs.

(a) Mitochondrial localization of PCDTs or PCDs by staining with Hoechst 33342 and Mito-tracker green. (b) Confocal images of MCF-7 cells with different treatment. ROS levels are determined using the fluorescent probe DCFH-DA. (c) Viability of MCF-7 cells with different treatments at various concentrations for 24 h under 808 nm NIR light irradiation. (d) Control experiments of cytotoxicity in MCF-7 cells at various concentrations for 24 h with or without NIR light irradiation.

(a) MMP analysis of MCF-7 cells using JC-1 mitochondrial membrane dye. (b) The analysis of caspase-3 activity in MCF-7 cells using FITC-conjugated caspase-3 antibody. (c) The detection of live/dead cells after various treatments. Live and dead cells are stained with calcein-AM (green) and PI (red), respectively. (d) Cell apoptosis analyzed by flow cytometry after different treatments using Annexin V/PI staining.

(a) Real-time in vivo fluorescence images after intravenous injection of PCDs or PCDTs in nude mice at different time points. (b) Ex vivo images of mice tissues (from top to bottom: heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidneys, tumor). The average fluorescence intensities from the (c) tumor area and (d) main organs for 24 h post-injection. The body weight (e), relative tumor volume (f), and tumor weight (g) of tumor-bearing mice with different treatments. (h) Representative tumor tissues stained with H&E, caspase-3, Ki67 and TUNEL assay after different treatments. Data are means ± SD; N = 5. ***P < 0.001.
Schematic illustration of the structure of PCDTs and their application to 808 nm NIR light-activated and mitochondria-targeted PDT.

head.ce:title: Engineering of upconversion carbon dots/metal-organic frameworks “Peeled Pitaya-Like” heterostructure for mitochondria-targeted photodynamic therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Marshall Laboratory of Biomedical Engineering, Research Center for Biosensor and Nanotheranostic, Guangdong Key Laboratory for Biomedical Measurements and Ultrasound Imaging, School of Biomedical Engineering, Health Science Center, Shenzhen University, Guangdong 518060, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Biosensor and Nanotheranostic
Guangdong Key Laboratory for Biomedical Measurements and Ultrasound Imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Marshall Laboratory of Biomedical Engineering, Research Center for Biosensor and Nanotheranostic, Guangdong Key Laboratory for Biomedical Measurements and Ultrasound Imaging, School of Biomedical Engineering, Health Science Center, Shenzhen University, Guangdong 518060, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Standardization and Measurement for Nanotechnology, CAS Center for Excellence in Nanoscience, National Center for Nanoscience and Technology, Beijing 100190, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Standardization and Measurement for Nanotechnology
CAS Center for Excellence in Nanoscience
National Center for Nanoscience and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Standardization and Measurement for Nanotechnology, CAS Center for Excellence in Nanoscience, National Center for Nanoscience and Technology, Beijing 100190, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing of near-infrared (NIR) light triggered metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) nano-photosensitizers (nPSs) is highly desirable for photodynamic therapy (PDT) owing to its promising properties. Herein, we designed a mitochondria-targeted, 808 nm near-infrared (NIR) light-triggered upconversion carbon dots (CDs) coordinated porphyrinic MOFs (PCDs) nPSs for enhanced PDT. The system was fabricated through incorporation of sufficient ultrasmall CDs into the nanopores as well as the surface of porphyrinic porous MOFs to construct a peeled pitaya-like heterostructure, which was further functionalized with mitochondria-targeted moieties of triphenylphosphines (TPPs), termed PCDTs, for enhanced PDT. The upconversion CDs act as tremendous “antennas” to efficiently absorb NIR laser, and the tight coupling between the CDs and porphyrin in MOFs, greatly shortened the distance between the two components and improved the efficiency of the energy transfer from the CDs to porphyrinic MOFs, thus yielded a sharply enhanced 1O2 generation capability. The mitochondria-targeted ability further enhanced the ROS oxidative stress, accelerated dysfunction of mitochondria, and induced intrinsic apoptosis. Excellent anticancer ability was demonstrated by the in vitro cancer cells killing and in vivo suppression of tumor growth. This work presents a novel engineered heterostructure to combat the current limitation of MOF in PDT.

ce:section.ce:para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is an extraordinary clinically approved treatment modality for cancer, in which a photosensitizer (PS) is sensitized by the wavelength-selected light, allowing for the generation of cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS) to locally destroy cancer cell-killing [1–8]. Porphyrins Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with tunable porosity, adjustable chemical compositions and controllable morphologies have emerged as desirable nano-photosensitizers (nPSs) for photodynamic therapy (PDT) [9–13], wherein the porous structures of MOFs are in favor of a high porphyrins loading content and an accelerated diffusion of ROS [5,14–18]. The bottle-neck that greatly hampered the PDT effect for porphyrinic nMOF is that most porphyrins could only be sensitized by ultraviolet (UV)/visible light, and the shallow penetration depth limit its in vivo application. Heterodimer [19] or core–shell structure [20] composed of upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) and nMOF were explored to achieve near-infrared (NIR) light-activated PDT, however it is still suffer from inefficient energy transfer because of the long distance between the two types of nanoparticles (NPs) within the heterodimer or sophisticated synthesis/modification process. Therefore, development of NIR light-activated porphyrinic nMOFs for amplified PDT is still highly desirable in current research.

Carbon dots (CDs) are considered as discrete quasi-spherical carbon NPs with ultrasmall size less than 10 nm, which exhibit broadband absorption and emission covering deep ultraviolet, visible and near-infrared range. It also displays excellent photoluminescence (PL) properties that can sequentially absorb two or more photons under long-wavelength radiation (e.g., NIR) and convert them into tunable, short-wavelength UV or visible emissions, which has received intense attention in biosensing [21,22], and bioimaging [23–26]. The unique photochemical characteristics of CDs make them promising materials for photo-mediated therapeutic applications. Meanwhile, the CDs can be easily synthesized from organic molecules by hydrothermal or solvothermal methods to generate various of carboxyl and hydroxyl group on the surface of CDs [27–30], giving rise to a high solubility and good biocompatibility for CDs without further surface modification. Therefore, it is practicable to functionalize MOF with CDs in consideration of its small-size effect and coordination interaction between the surface oxygen related groups of CDs and coordinatively unsaturated metal sites of MOF domain, but it is never been explored to the best of our knowledge.

Herein, we design a upconversion nPS composed of porous PCN-244 MOF decorated with tremendous upconversion CDs in both pores and surface for high-performance mitochondria-targeted amplified PDT. The ultrasmall CDs was synthesized through a facile one-step hydrothermal route, which possessed excellent upconversion PL property. It was easily admitted into the nanopores and channels as well as the surface of porous MOFs to construct a “peeled pitaya-like” heterostructure for a shortened distance between CDs and porphyrins, on which surface was further modified with the mitochondria-targeted ligands of triphenylphosphines (TPPs) to generate PCDTs. The coordination interactions between the oxygen-containing functional groups of CDs and unsaturated metal sites of MOF gave a high stability for the heterostructure. The resulting PCDTs possessed remarkable absorption in the NIR region resulted from the numerous CDs “antennas”, and allow efficient energy transfer to the MOF in proximity, leading to generation of a large amount of cytotoxic ROS to achieve NIR triggered PDT. The mitochondria-targeted ability further enhanced elevated ROS press, leading to accelerated dysfunction of mitochondria for an amplified PDT (Scheme 1 ).

CDs were synthesized by low-temperature aqueous heating [1]. 0.1761 g of ascorbic acid was dissolved in 19 mL water under ultrasonication, followed by adding 1 mL Cu(Ac)2 solution (0.1 M). Afterwards, the product was stirring at 90 °C for 5 h, and then centrifuged to reserve the supernatant.

50 mg of H2TCPP, 150 mg of ZrOCl2·8H2O, and 1.4 g of benzoic acid were dissolved in 100 mL of DMF and stirring at 90 °C for 5 h. Then, the resulting products were collected by centrifugation and washing with DMF for several times.

CDs solution (10 mg mL−1) was added into the suspension of PCN-224 (5 mg mL−1). After sonication for 30 min and stirring at 25℃ for 24 h, the resulting mixture were lyophilized to give a brown product (PCDs). Wherein, the mass ratio of PCN-224 to CDs is controlled at 1: 1. TPP conjugation to the PCDs was performed by mixing 0.5 mL of TPP solution with 0.5 mL of PCDs samples (mass ratio of PCDs: TPP was 12:1). The mixture was stirred 24 h at 25℃ and the resulting materials was dialysed and finally lyophilized to obtain PCDTs.

0.1 mg of the samples was suspended in 1 mL of water containing 5 m M of DPBF, then the mixture was kept in the dark or irradiated with 808 nm laser for different time periods with the power of 1 W cm−2. The absorption spectrum of solution was obtained by UV–vis detection.

0.1 mg of the samples was dissolved in 1 mL of water containing 5 μM of SOSG. The mixture was kept in the dark or irradiated by 808 nm NIR laser for different time with the power of 1 Wcm−2. A fluorescence spectrophotometer was utilized to monitor the fluorescence emission of SOSG.

0.1 mg of the samples was dissolved in 1 mL of water containing 1 m M of capturing agent. 808 nm laser was utilized to irradiate the mixed liquid with the power of 1 Wcm−2. Then a EPR spectrometer was utilized to detection.

MCF-7 cells were obtained from the Cell Bank of Shanghai Institute of Biochemistry and Cell Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences. And cells were cultured in DMEM medium supplemented with 10% (v/v) FBS and 1% antibiotics (penicillin/streptomycin, 100 U mL−1) at 37 °C under a 5% CO2 atmosphere.

The MCF-7 cells were seeded in 96-well plate for 24 h, then different concentrations of samples were added for 4 h. Afterward, the cells were kept in the dark or irradiated with 808 nm laser for 10 min (1 W cm−2). After another incubation of 24 h, 100 μL fresh culture medium containing 10% CCK-8 was added. The absorbance was measured by a microplate reader at the wavelength of 450 nm.

MCF-7 cells were cultivated overnight in a glass bottom dish, then treated with various nanoparticles for 4 h. After irradiated with 808 nm NIR laser for 10 min (1 W cm−2), cellular ROS were detected using a ROS fluorescence probe (2,7-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate, DCFH-DA,10 μM) and observed by CLSM.

MCF-7 cells were cultivated overnight in a glass bottom dish, then PCDs or PCDTs (100 μg mL−1) was added and incubation for 4 h. Afterwards, the cells were dyed by Hoechst 33342 and the Mito-Tracker Green, respectively, and imaged by CLSM. Since the fluorescent feature of PCN-224, no additional modification is required.

MCF-7 cells were cultivated overnight in a glass bottom dish, then PCDs or PCDTs (100 μg mL−1) was added and incubation for 4 h. After washing, the cells incubated with culture medium containing 1.0 μg mL−1 of JC-1 dye for 15 min, and visualized under CLSM.

MCF-7 cells were cultivated overnight in a glass bottom dish, then PCDs or PCDTs (100 μg mL−1) was added and incubation for 4 h, and then irradiated with 808 nm NIR laser for 10 min (1 Wcm−2). Afterward, cells were fixed with 4% of paraformaldehyde, permeabilized with 0.1% Triton X-100, and were blocked with 5% of BSA for 1 h, stained with cleaved caspase-3 antibody at 4 °C, and dyed by FITC conjugated secondary antibody for 1 h and imaged by CLSM.

MCF-7 cells were cultivated overnight in a glass bottom dish, then PCDs or PCDTs (100 μg mL−1) was added and incubation for 4 h. And staining with calcein AM (2 × 10−3 M) and propidium iodide PI (5 × 10−3 M) for 10 min, respectively, and imaged by CLSM.

MCF-7 cells were cultivated overnight in a glass bottom dish, The different samples (50 µg mL−1) were added. After 4 h incubation, the cells were irradiated by 808 nm NIR laser for 10 min (1 Wcm−2). After 24 h incubation, cells were stained for flow cytometric analysis.

All animal experiments were performed under protocols approved by the Department of Laboratory Animal Science of Peking University Health Science Center. Female Balb/C mice (18–22 g) were bought from Beijing Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology Co., Ltd. MCF-7 cells (3 × 106 cells 100 μL−1) were injected subcutaneously into the right hind leg of the mouse. When the size of tumors grew to 90–130 mm3, the mice were divided randomly into seven groups (n = 5 mouse per group) for the treatment with (I) none, (II) PBS, (III) PBS + NIR, (IV) PCDs, (V) PCDTs, (VI) PCDs + NIR, (VII) PCDTs + NIR (PCDs or PCDTs dose: 20 mg kg−1). NIR treatment was performed 12 h post-injection by irradiating the tumor regions with 808 nm laser for 10 min (1.0 W cm−2, 3 min interval for every 1 min of laser irradiation was used to avoid potential heating effect). After treatments, the tumor volume and the body weight of mice were measured every other day during 2 weeks.
After two weeks of continuous treatments, the mice were euthanized and the primary organs and tumor tissues were sliced and stained for histological analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo imaging and biodistribution analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In vivo fluorescence images were obtained through employing a vivo imaging system (IVIS Spectrum system). The mice were anesthetized and detected by fluorescence in vivo in different time periods after intravenous injection of PCDs or PCDTs nanocatalysts (100 μg mL−1,100 μL). After that, the mice were dissected in time and removed from the heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney and tumor for fluorescence detection.

MCF-7 cancer-bearing mice were intravenously injected with PCDs or PCDTs (20 mg kg−1). After intravenous injection for 24 h, the mice were sacrificed to collect the liver, spleen, kidney, heart, lung, and tumor. Then, the Zr amount of the collected tissue samples was measured by ICP-MS.

The in vivo toxicity of PCDs and PCDTs was preliminarily evaluated by a blood routine test (BRT). In brief, PCDs and PCDTs (20 mg kg−1) was i.v. injected into ten healthy Balb/c mice in two groups. After 1, 7, and 14 days injection, all five mice in a group were sacrificed to collect blood (0.8 mL) for BRT, respectively. Five other healthy Balb/c mice without any treat were used as the controls.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of PCDs and PCDTs
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication of CDs/MOF are illustrated in Fig. 1 a. Firstly, CDs are synthesized through a hydrothermal route with L-ascorbic acid as a carbon source, and Cu2+ as a catalyst for the reaction [31] (Fig. S1).The resulting CDs appeared as spherical particles with a mean diameter of 2 nm and could well be dispersed in aqueous solution (Fig. 1d). The high-resolution transmission electron microscope image (HRTEM) of CDs shows the interplanar spacing of 0.208 nm (the inset in Fig. 1d), which is indexed as both the (103) lattice fringe of sp3 carbon and the (102) lattice fringe of the sp2 carbon [32]. And the CDs exhibited unique characteristic excitation and pH dependent PL properties [33] (Fig. S2). The synthesized PCN-224 s [34] displayed a spherical morphology with a mean diameter of ∼ 56 nm measured by the TEM (Fig. S3a). After conjugating with CDs, the morphologies of PCDs had no obvious change, presenting good dispersity and uniformity (Fig. S3b and S3c), as well as a good optical stability (Fig. S4). Obviously, there are numerous of CDs decorated on the MOF (Fig. 1b and c). The element mapping revealed even distribution of Zr, C and N elements in the PCDs (Fig. 1h). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) also confirmed the successful decoration of CDs on the PCN-224 (Fig. S5). And the dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis demonstrated that the size distribution of the PCN-224, PCDs and PCDTs were 78.8 ± 1, 91.3 ± 2, and 122.4 ± 2 nm, respectively (Fig. 1e), and Zeta potentials depicted the corresponding zeta potential of −10.8 mV, 29 mV, −3.6 mV and 9.4 mV for CDs, PCN-224, PCDs and PCDTs (Fig. 1f), proving that the PCN-224 have been successfully modified with CDs and TPP to generate PCDs and PCDTs. It was worthy to mention that the CDs with the negative terminal oxygen-containing groups [35] (Fig. S7, Table S2) could be well coupled onto the PCN-224 that possesses a positive charge. Besides, the interaction between the oxygen-containing groups of CDs and the coordinatively unsaturated metal sites in PCN-224 endowed the PCDs with strong stability in water, PBS, DMEM and FBS (Fig. S8), which provided great potential for its in vitro and in vivo application. And FTIR spectrum is investigated to acquire further structural insights about the obtained nanomaterials. As described in Fig. 1e, all the characteristic peaks including C=C group at 1650 cm−1 and O–H, C–O/C–C groups at 1000–1400 cm−1 in PCN-224 and TPP appeared in PCDTs, as well as the COOH group of CDs included in PCDTs at 1740 cm−1, suggesting that CDs and TPP have been successfully conjugated with the PCN-224 to yield the PCDTs.

More importantly, the obtained CDs displayed an efficient upconversion luminescence (UCL) that presented emission in the range of 400–650 nm under the irradiation of an 808 nm laser as shown in Fig. 2 a, which was originated from the multiphoton active process [36–42]. The emission spectrum overlapped well with the absorption spectrum of the PCN-224, facilitated the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from the CDs to PCN-224 within PCDs. Compared with CDs, the PCDs revealed a dramatic shortening in the PL lifetime at 520 nm evaluated by time-resolved PL measurements (Fig. 2b), which further validated an efficient energy transfer from the CDs to PCN-224. The ROS generation from the porphyrinic PCN-224 in PCDs activated by the FRET under a NIR light irradiation was assessed using SOSG (an indicator of 1O2). There was negligible fluorescence for the SOSG alone, CDs and PCN-224 under an 808 nm laser irradiation. A remarkable increase of the SOSG fluorescence around 530 nm was recorded for PCDs upon an 808 nm laser irradiation (Fig. 2c), which indicated strong NIR activated 1O2 generation ability of the PCDs. The generation of 1O2 was also detected by monitoring the change of the absorption of DPBF solution mixture in different condition, it is cleared that the absorption peaks of DPBF in the presence of PCDs show an obvious decline with the prolonged radiation time, suggesting that the PCDs under an 808 nm laser irradiation would induce numerous 1O2 generation (Fig. S9), which was great agreement with the result of SOSG analysis”. Electron spin resonance (ESR) further confirmed the excellent 1O2 generation ability of PCDs under an 808 nm laser irradiation (Fig. 2d). These results demonstrated that the energy efficiently transferred from the CDs to the porphyrinic PCN-224 that enabled the activation of PCN-224 to generate 1O2 under a NIR light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NIR-triggered energy transfer and according mechanism explanation
ce:section.ce:para: Such a high-efficiency energy transfer within PCDs was resulted from the fact that CDs could not only attach onto the external surface of PCN-224 by the coordination interaction, but also enter the pores of the framework. It could be well demonstrated by N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms of PCN-224 and PCDs. In comparison with pristine PCN-224, the PCDs showed a dramatical reduction in both specific surface area and pore volume (Fig. 2e, f and Table S3), confirming that CDs have been effectively incorporated to the framework of PCN-224 to form a pitaya-like heterostructure. The resulting pitaya-like structures greatly shorten the distance between CDs and porphyrin molecules within PCDs, yielding the improved FRET efficiency from the CDs to the porphyrinic PCN-224, contributing to an elevated 1O2 production efficiency. We also evaluated the FRET effect within PCDs in living cells. As shown in Fig. S10, the CDs-incubated MCF-7 cells treated with the NIR irradiation displayed a bright green fluorescence. After the CDs assembly with PCN-224, the green fluorescence of CDs decreased along with the appearance of red fluorescence of porphyrin molecules within PCN-224, further confirming that the photon energy absorbed by CDs could be efficiently transferred to the porphyrinic PCN-224.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of ROS generation, mitochondrial targeting and damage
ce:section.ce:para: The PCDs are subsequently modified with the TPP molecules to give a mitochondria-targeting capability for the resulted PCDTs [43–47]. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence of PCDTs in the MCF-7 cells matched well with the fluorescence of Mito-Tracker Red. In contrast, PCDs match poorly with Mito-Tracker Red (Fig. S11). These results indicated that the good cumulation of PCDTs into mitochondria. Next, the ability of PCDs and PCDTs to trigger 1O2 generation in cells are assessed with a fluorogenic indicator of 2,7-dichlorofluorescein-diacetate (DCFH-DA). As revealed in Fig. 3b, the cells incubated with nonirradiated PCDTs displayed a negligible fluorescence of DCF. After an 808 nm laser irradiation, a bright green fluorescence of DCF has occurred in both cells incubated with PCDTs or PCDs, further confirmed the efficient energy transfer triggered by NIR light. And the fluorescence intensity of DCF in cells incubated with PCDTs was 2.3-folds higher than that incubated with PCDs by quantitative analysis (Fig. S12), this may be attributed to the positively charged PCDTs could work better for endocytosis than PCDs which carried negative charge.

The cell viability assay (CCK-8 assay) has confirmed that all the NPs incubated with cells exhibited little cytotoxicity with the cell viabilities above 89 % even at a high concentration of 200 μg mL−1 for 48 h in the absence of irradiation (Fig. 3d and Fig. S13). No obvious cell-killing effects were observed for cells incubated with CDs or PCN-224 with the NIR light irradiation (Fig. 3c,d). Upon an 808 nm laser irradiation, the cell viability of PCDTs-treated cells decreased to 15%, which was 1.7 times lower than that of PCDs. All these results suggested that the mitochondria-targeting ability of PCDTs enabled the generation of ROS in mitochondria to amplify the PDT effect.

The change of mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) was evaluated by JC-1 staining [48,49]. The PCDTs-treated cells without irradiation revealed an imperceptible green fluorescence (Fig. 4 a). After an 808 nm laser irradiation, the increased ratio of green-to-red signal in both cells treated with PCDTs and PCDs was obviously detected, indicating a severe mitochondria damage with a depolarized MMP [50–52]. It revealed that the ratio of green-to-red in PCDTs-treated cells was 5.4-fold higher than that of PCDs-treated cells (Fig. S14). And, the activity of caspase-3 was also evaluated to characterize cells apoptosis in different treatments [52].

The expression of caspase-3 increased remarkably after NIR irradiation in PCDTs groups compared to that without irradiation (Fig. 4b). As a control, the PCDs treatment with NIR irradiation exerted less effect on the caspase-3 activation (Fig. S15), indicating the mitochondria-mediated mechanism of apoptosis [53]. These results were further supported by Calcein-AM/PI double stain assay and the Annexin V-FITC/PI apoptosis detection assay (Fig. 4c, d and Fig. S16). As expected, the PCDTs treatment with an 808 nm laser irradiation group exhibited the least live cells among all the groups. All these results confirmed that the 1O2 generation in mitochondria could greatly amplify cell apoptosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The biosafety, biodistribution and fluorescence imaging of PCDTs
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, using MCF-7 tumor-bearing mice as a model, the performance of PCDTs to suppress tumor growth in vivo was investigated. Firstly, the biodistribution of PCDTs was monitored via in vivo fluorescence imaging. As presented in Fig. 5 a, after intravenous (i.v.) injection of PCDTs and PCDs, the NPs were distributed throughout the whole body with bright fluorescence signals at 1 h post-injection, indicating that both PCDs and PCDTs have good dispersibility and stability in tissue. The tumor-sites of both PCDs and PCDTs groups showed the prominent signals than other parts of the body with the time increase increased to 24 h, manifesting the good tumor accumulation of the NPs through the blood circulation and an enhanced permeability retention (EPR) effect [54–57]. After 12 h injection, the fluorescence signals in both groups weakened indicated that the NPs could be excreted from the body (Fig. 5c, Fig. S17a). At 24 h post-injection, the organs of injected mice were excised for ex vivo imaging (Fig. 5b), which demonstrated that the fluorescence signals of the tumors were much higher than that of other organs. The quantitative data revealed the fluorescence intensity in the tumor was 4.7-folds higher than that in the liver for PCDTs injected mice (Fig. 5d), indicating that PCDTs have an excellent tumor targeting capability. The similar result could also be obtained by the ICP-MS measure of Zr in tumors and major organs resected from mice of PCDTs or PCDs group (Fig. S17b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo tumor therapy studies and histological analysis
ce:section.ce:para: According to in vivo fluorescence imaging analysis, 12 h was the appropriate time to perform NIR laser irradiation. The tumor-bearing mice are randomly divided into seven groups: Control (I), PBS (II), PBS + NIR (III), PCDs (IV), PCDTs (V), PCDs + NIR (VI), and PCDTs + NIR (VII). There were little weights loss for all groups at 14 days after PDT administration, indicating negligible adverse effects of the NPs (Fig. 5e). Compared with the PCDs + NIR or PCDTs + NIR treated mice, tumors grow aggressively in all other groups in term of tumors volume and weight (Fig. 5f, g, Fig. S18). Particularly, the PCDTs + NIR (VII) group showed most tumor regression as seen in the representative photographs (Fig. S19) of the final tumors resected from mice of each group. It revealed an enhanced PDT efficacy of PCDTs induced by an amplified oxidative stress in mitochondria.

The antitumor effect was further supported by tumor-pathological analysis [58–64]. The hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining presented severe cellular necrosis and apoptosis both in PCDs + NIR and PCDTs + NIR-treated tumors in comparison to those in other groups (Fig. 5h). Additionally, Ki67 expression decreased drastically while the caspase-3 expression and TUNEL (deoxynucleotidyl-transferase-mediated nick end labelling) signal increased greatly both in PCDs + NIR and PCDTs + NIR -treated tumors relative to other groups. These results validate that the NIR light-activated PDT to induced cell apoptosis effectively. The treatment with PCDTs + NIR led to the more severe apoptosis of tumor cells that that of PCDs + NIR group, indicating the superior PDT effect by the mitochondrial targeting effect. Moreover, no cytotoxicity of NPs was found even at a high dose of 20 mg kg−1 by the measure of standard blood biochemical indexes (Fig. S20), blood routine indexes (Fig. S21) as well as the H&E staining assay of the major organs of all groups (Fig. S22).

In summary, a peeled pitaya-like upconversion carbon dots incorporated metal–organic frameworks heterostructure PS, termed as PCDs, has been designed, on which surface was modified with TTP (PCDTs) for mitochondria-targeting amplified PDT. Owing to the small size of CDs and the coordination interaction between the oxygen-containing functional groups of CDs and unsaturated metal sites of MOF, tremendous of CDs were incorporated into the internal pore and external surface of the MOF. It showed stable PL, excellent water dispersibility and good biocompatibility. Under NIR laser irradiation, the abundant CDs acted as “antenna” to absorb NIR laser to effectively transfer the energy to porphyrinic MOF due to the tight coupling and short distance, leading to excellent 1O2 generation efficacy for PDT. The mitochondria targeting ability of PCDTs further amplified the ROS press for therapy. Therefore, a desirable in vitro and in vivo cancer cell-killing performance was achieved. This work provides a novel and versatile pitaya-like heterostructure for mitochondria-targeted amplified PDT, especially contributing to the rational design of nPS nanostructure to combat the limitation in PDT.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Excellent Young Scientists Fund (22022407), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874008, 22004006), the work was also supported by Major Program of National Natural Science Foundation of China (21890740 and 21890742) and Special Foundation for State Major Research Program of China (Grant Nos.2019YFC1606603), SZU Top Ranking Project (860000002100165, 86000000210) and Beijing Municipa Science and Technology Commission (Grant No. z131102002813058). 2021 Stability support plan of Shenzhen University (8940206/0200).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.136706.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
